title: Tamil Pavakkai Pitlai

ingredients.name: bitter gourd (pavakkai), sliced in rounds
visual_checks: rub with 1 tsp salt, rest 10 min, squeeze and rinse to reduce bitterness
ingredients.notes: rub with 1 tsp salt, rest 10 min, squeeze and rinse to reduce bitterness

subs.name: brinjal, cubed — the dish becomes a standard pitlai without the bitterness
toor dal — gives a softer, creamier texture

ingredients.name: tamarind
visual_checks: soak in 200 ml warm water, extract pulp
ingredients.notes: soak in 200 ml warm water, extract pulp

subs.name: 3 tbsp coconut milk powder blended to a thick paste

steps.text: Rub the bitter gourd slices with 1 teaspoon of salt, rest for 10 minutes, then squeeze firmly and rinse under cold water. This removes some of the bitterness. Pressure cook the chana dal with 1 cup water and turmeric for 3 whistles until cooked through but not mushy.

Soak the tamarind in 200 ml warm water and extract the pulp. Dry-roast the coriander seeds in a dry pan for 1 minute until fragrant. Grind together the roasted coriander, coconut, black pepper, and 2 dried red chillies with a little water into a smooth thick paste.

Heat oil in a kadai over medium flame. Splutter mustard seeds, add urad dal and fry until golden. Add the remaining dried red chilli, curry leaves, asafoetida, and shallots. Cook the shallots until they begin to soften, about 3 minutes.

Add the bitter gourd pieces and stir-fry on medium heat for 5 minutes until they soften slightly. Pour in the tamarind extract, add salt, and bring to a boil. Simmer for 10 minutes uncovered until the bitter gourd is tender and the raw tamarind smell cooks off.

Add the cooked chana dal and the coconut-spice paste. Stir well and simmer for another 8 minutes until the pitlai thickens and the coconut paste is fully cooked — a raw coconut smell means it needs more time.

Check salt and sourness. The pitlai should be moderately thick with a complex flavour — sour, slightly bitter, and richly spiced. Serve with steamed rice; a spoonful of ghee stirred in at the table is traditional and excellent.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Follow the cooker manufacturer's fill, venting and opening instructions; never force the lid open under pressure.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: bitter-gourd → brinjal, cubed — the dish becomes a standard pitlai without the bitterness; chana-dal → toor dal — gives a softer, creamier texture; coconut → 3 tbsp coconut milk powder blended to a thick paste
